ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists for cream-topped Malaysian coffee specifically; closest parent 'coffee beverage' (FOODON_03301710) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code AN0KL (Coffee, with milk or cream) is the closest parent; no specific code for cream-crowned Malaysian-style coffee exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 34731007 (Coffee substance) used as closest parent; no specific concept for cream-topped coffee beverage exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately one cup (250 g / 250 ml) of Cream Crown Coffee as typically served at a Malaysian-style coffee bar.

nutrient_source.title: Coffee, brewed from grounds, prepared with tap water (USDA FoodData Central ID 171890) and Heavy cream (USDA FoodData Central ID 170859); composite estimate for cream-topped coffee beverage
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Cream Crown Coffee exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or USDA Branded Foods. Nutrient values are estimated from a composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for brewed coffee (FDC ID 171890) and heavy whipping cream (FDC ID 170859), weighted to reflect an approximate 200 ml brewed coffee base with 50 ml heavy cream topping, adjusted to per-100 g basis. Sugar contribution estimated from typical Malaysian kopi preparation with sweetened condensed milk component. Values represent a reasonable central estimate; actual values will vary by recipe.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J; coffee and dairy-based beverages reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Cream Crown Coffee. GI estimated at 35 based on analogous coffee-with-cream beverages from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables. Brewed coffee has a GI of approximately 0 (negligible carbohydrate); the cream component contributes fat and minimal carbohydrate (lactose GI ~46); sweetened condensed milk component raises GI slightly. Composite GI of 35 is a defensible low-GI estimate. GL per 250 g serving = (35 × 3.2 × 2.5) / 100 = 2.8, rounded to one decimal place.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~20 mg per 250 ml serving)
hypertension.guidance: Cream Crown Coffee contains approximately 20 mg sodium per serving, which is low and unlikely to significantly affect blood pressure; however, individuals with hypertension should be aware that caffeine may cause a transient acute rise in blood pressure and should moderate overall daily coffee intake to 1–2 cups.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar from sweetened condensed milk and moderate fat from cream
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cream Crown Coffee has a low estimated GI of 35 and a glycemic load of approximately 2.8 per serving, making it relatively low-impact glycaemically; however, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request reduced or no sweetened condensed milk to minimise added sugar intake and monitor portion size given the caloric contribution of heavy cream.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from heavy cream (~8 g per 250 ml serving)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The heavy cream component of Cream Crown Coffee contributes approximately 8 g of saturated fat per serving, which represents a meaningful proportion of the recommended daily saturated fat limit (~20 g for a 2000 kcal diet); individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption or request a reduced-cream preparation to manage saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cream Crown Coffee is a premium Malaysian-style coffee beverage served at specialty coffee bars such as EC Coffee Bar on Carpenter Street in Singapore's Chinatown district. It consists of a base of strong brewed coffee — typically prepared from robusta or arabica beans in the traditional Malaysian kopi style — topped with a generous crown of lightly whipped or poured heavy cream that floats on the surface, creating a visually striking two-layer presentation. The drink is typically sweetened with sweetened condensed milk or sugar, giving it a rich, slightly caramelised sweetness that contrasts with the bitter coffee base. The cream crown melts gradually into the coffee as it is consumed, producing a progressively richer, creamier flavour profile. Texturally, the beverage transitions from a smooth, dense cream layer to a warm, full-bodied coffee beneath. Nutritionally, the heavy cream component contributes significant fat (predominantly saturated) and calories, while the coffee base provides caffeine, potassium, and trace B vitamins. The estimated glycemic index is low (~35) due to the fat-dominant cream component slowing carbohydrate absorption, though added sugar from condensed milk raises the glycemic load modestly. Clinically, individuals managing cardiovascular disease or Type 2 Diabetes should moderate intake due to saturated fat and added sugar content. The beverage is halal-suitable and vegetarian-friendly, and is typically served hot in a glass or ceramic cup.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Cream Crown Coffee exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or any approved primary regional database. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite of brewed coffee and heavy cream components, weighted by typical recipe proportions. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for similar cream-coffee beverages. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Cream Crown Coffee is a specialty beverage item from EC Coffee Bar (Carpenter Street, Singapore), sourced from field survey data. No direct nutritional entry exists in approved primary sources; all nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (brewed coffee FDC 171890 + heavy cream FDC 170859) adjusted to a typical 200 ml coffee + 50 ml cream recipe, expressed per 100 g. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — coffee beverages with cream contain no dietary fibre). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no vitamin C in coffee or cream). vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA composite source at the precision required. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported separately in the composite estimate. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the source. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. trans_fat_g value of 0.1 reflects naturally occurring trans fats from dairy (vaccenic acid) per USDA data. GI set to 35 as an analogous estimate for cream-coffee beverage; gi_category is low (GI ≤ 55). GL per 250 g serving = (35 × 3.2 × 2.5) / 100 = 2.8. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean cuisine category (English, Chinese Simplified, Chinese Traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) per G6 rules. The cuisine tag 'Cake' in the input source data appears to be a field survey platform categorisation artefact and is not reflected in the food classification as no cake component is confirmed in the beverage.
